content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

November 22, 2016

Melinda C. H. Stewart
City Attorney
City Attorney's Office
650 Merchant Street
Vacaville, CA 95688

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-16-169

Dear Ms. Stewart:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

QUESTIONS

1. May you work on a contract amendment to extend a lease with Comcast of California/Massachusetts/Michigan/Utah, LLC ("Comcast") for communications facilities that is subject to city council approval despite the fact that your dependent daughter has earned a $1,000 college scholarship from Comcast?

2. May you draft or assist with drafting an ordinance regulating cable television operations in the City of Vacaville that would be subject to city council approval in light of your daughter's receipt of the Comcast scholarship?

CONCLUSIONS

1 & 2. Yes. You may participate in both decisions because your dependent daughter's receipt of a scholarship from Comcast is not income to you and, therefore, you would not have a conflict of interest in the governmental decisions described.

_________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-16-169
Page No. 2

FACTS

You are the City Attorney for the City of Vacaville and are a Section 87200 filer. Your
daughter was recently awarded a $1,000 "Leaders and Achievers Scholarship" from the Comcast
Foundation to be used for college tuition. The check is made out directly to her college. Your
daughter is 17 years old and a dependent. After bring nominated by her school administration, your
daughter was required to complete an application which included short answer responses to
questions and may have included letters of recommendation and/or a personal statement. According
to the Comcast website the scholarship is administered independently by International Scholarship
and Tuition Services. The scholarship requirements include:

• Full time high school student currently enrolled in a high school in a community served or
approved by Comcast.
• Student grade point average of 2.8 or higher.
• Student demonstrates leadership abilities in school activities or through work experiences.
• Student shows a strong commitment and involvement in community service.
• Student plans to attend an accredited, non-profit college, university or vocational·technical
school in the United States.

You would like to know whether your daughter's receipt of the Comcast scholarship creates
a conflict of interest that would prohibit you from participating in governmental decisions that
involve an amendment to extend a lease with Comcast and an ordinance regulating cable television
operations.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the official's financial interests. (Section 87103.)

Under the Act financial interests include:

• Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest of at least
$2,000. (Section 87103(b).)

• Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth at
least $2,000 and any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer,
partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(a) and (d).)

• Any source of income, such as a business entity, from which the official has received
income of $500 or more within 12 months before the decision. This also includes income
from any client of the business entity of at least $ 500, provided to and received by the

File No. A-16-169
Page No. 3

public official within 12 months before the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).)

• Any donor of gift(s) amounting to a total of at least $460 within 12 months before a decision is made. (Section 87103(e).)

• A public official's own personal finances, or those of a member of his or her immediate family. (Section 87103).)

The Act defines "gift" in Section 82028(a) as "any payment that confers a personal benefit on the recipient, to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received and includes a rebate or discount in the price of anything of value unless the rebate or discount is made in the regular course of business to members of the public without regard to official status." Under some circumstances a gift to an official's immediate family member is considered a gift to the official. Immediate family includes dependent children (See Regulation 18943). The Act's broad definition of gift has several exceptions to allow for day-to-day business and practicalities of public office. In your case the scholarship was earned by your daughter through a bona fide competition so her receipt of the scholarship would not be considered a gift to you.

The "bona fide competition" is an exception to the "gift" rule. (Regulation 18942(a)(14).) A scholarship or other prize that a public official gains in a bona fide competition, rather with regard to his or her official status, is not a gift and is reported as income. (Regulation 18942(a)(14).) The scholarship must be the same for the public official (or his or her immediate family) as would be offered any others. One of the main factors in making this determination is the nature of the pool of contestants in the competition and the specific circumstances of the award. For instance, in a previous advice letter, we advised that a "Woman of the Year" award provided to Assemblymember Jackie Speier qualified as a bona fide competition unrelated to her official status since an independent panel of judges selected her from of a large pool of contestants representing the fields of medicine, sports, social work, journalism and public service. (Pritchard Advice Letter, No. A-95-094; See also Maitland Advice Letter, A-99-158, where official was advised that a fellowship offered to her as a mid-career professional in a public policy field was an award received in a bona fide competition.)

In this case your daughter applied for a scholarship after being nominated by her school. The eligibility criteria used by Comcast to determine who the scholarship recipient would be was broad enough to include a large pool of contestants. There is no indication that your daughter received the award due to your status as a public official, but rather she earned the scholarship based upon merit. Therefore, the scholarship is not considered to be a gift to her or you under the Act. However, a scholarship to an official that is received as a result of a bona fide competition would be as income instead of a gift under Regulation 18942(a)(14).

"Income" is defined in Section 82030 as a payment received by a public official and includes the official's community property interest in the income of a spouse. In your case, it was your dependent daughter that received a scholarship from Comcast. The definition of income under the Act does not include income received by an official's dependent children. Therefore, the scholarship was not income to you as defined under the Act.

File No. A-16-169
Page No. 4

You have not identified any other financial interest that could be affected under the Act. Therefore, you would not have a conflict of interest in participating in the lease extension amendment with Comcast or the cable television ordinance due to your daughter's receipt of a scholarship from Comcast.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: Sukhi K. Brar
Counsel, Legal Division

SKB:jgl

Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or 
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the 
official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental 
decision if it is reasonab(v foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one

• Any source of income, such as a business entity, from which the official has received
